title: Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć
summary:
Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć (also spelled Pu‚Äėu‚ÄėŇć‚ÄėŇć, and often written Puu Oo, pronounced [ňąpu Ēu ňą Ēoňź Ēoňź], poo-oo-OH-oh) is a volcanic cone on the eastern rift zone of Kńęlauea volcano in the Hawaiian Islands. The eruption that created Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć began on January 3, 1983, and continued nearly continuously until April 30, 2018, making it the longest-lived rift-zone eruption of the last two centuries.
By January 2005, 2.7 cubic kilometers (0.65 mi3) of magma covered an area of more than 117 square kilometers (45 mi2) and added 230 acres (0.93 km2) of land to the southeast coast of Hawai Ľi. The eruption claimed at least 189 buildings and 14 kilometers (8.7 mi) of highways, as well as a church, a store, the Waha Ľula Visitor Center, and many ancient Hawaiian sites, including the Waha Ľula heiau. The coastal highway has been closed since 1987, as parts of the road have been buried under lava up to 35 meters (115 ft) thick.
text:
Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć (also spelled Pu‚Äėu‚ÄėŇć‚ÄėŇć, and often written Puu Oo, pronounced [ňąpu Ēu ňą Ēoňź Ēoňź], poo-oo-OH-oh) is a volcanic cone on the eastern rift zone of Kńęlauea volcano in the Hawaiian Islands. The eruption that created Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć began on January 3, 1983, and continued nearly continuously until April 30, 2018, making it the longest-lived rift-zone eruption of the last two centuries.
By January 2005, 2.7 cubic kilometers (0.65 mi3) of magma covered an area of more than 117 square kilometers (45 mi2) and added 230 acres (0.93 km2) of land to the southeast coast of Hawai Ľi. The eruption claimed at least 189 buildings and 14 kilometers (8.7 mi) of highways, as well as a church, a store, the Waha Ľula Visitor Center, and many ancient Hawaiian sites, including the Waha Ľula heiau. The coastal highway has been closed since 1987, as parts of the road have been buried under lava up to 35 meters (115 ft) thick.

Etymology
The hill was initially nicknamed "Pu Ľu O" by volcanologists, as its position when marked on a map of the area coincided with an "o" in "Lava flow of 1965". Later, the elders of the village of Kalapana were asked to name the new hill, and chose Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć, meaning hill of the digging stick. The name is also often translated as "Hill of the  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć Bird". In 2021, the Hawai Ľi Board of Geographic Names updated the spelling of the cone as Pu‚Äėu‚ÄėŇć‚ÄėŇć for consistency with the board's spelling guidelines. The Hawaiian Volcano Observatory began following the new recommended naming convention shortly thereafter.

Geologic history
1983‚Äď1986: High lava fountains build tall cinder and spatter cone
The Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć eruption began when fissures split the ground in the remote rainforest of the eastern rift zone, on January 3, 1983. By June 1983, the activity had strengthened and localized to the Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć vent. Over the next three years, 44 eruptive episodes with lava fountains as high as 460 meters (1,510 ft) stopped traffic at points across east Hawai Ľi. The fallout of cinder and spatter from the towering lava fountains built a cone 255 meters (837 ft) high.

1986‚Äď1991: Eruption shifts east to KŇępa ĽianahńĀ
In July 1986, the conduit feeding magma to Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć ruptured, and the eruption abruptly shifted 3 kilometers (1.9 mi) downrift to form the KŇępa ĽianahńĀ vent. With the new vent came a new style of eruption: continuous, quiet effusion from a lava lake replaced the episodic high fountaining. After a few weeks, a roof formed over the main lava outflow channel, which created a lava tube. The lava tube allowed the fluid pahoehoe lava to retain heat and flow long distances. In less than a year, overflow from the lake created a broad and low shield about 55 meters (180 ft) above KŇępa ĽianahńĀ.
Lava streams were first visible from the town of Kapa Ľau in November, 1986. In the course of that month, lava cut a swath through Kapa Ľahu, covered the coastal highway, and finally reached the ocean 12 kilometers (7.5 mi) from the vent. Some weeks later, the lava flow shifted eastwards and buried 14 houses in the town of Kalapana within one day. The lava flow at Kalapana ceased when the lava tube system shut down.

In 1990, the eruption entered its most destructive phase, when flows turned eastward and completely destroyed the villages of Kalapana and KaimŇę. KaimŇę Bay and Kalapana Black Sand Beach were also completely covered with lava. Over 100 homes were destroyed by the ever-broadening flow field in a nine-month period. New tubes diverted lava away from Kalapana early in 1991, and lava once again entered the ocean within Hawaii Volcanoes National Park.

1992‚Äď2013: Steady lava effusion
The volume of lava erupted from KŇępa ĽianahńĀ declined steadily through 1991, and in early 1992, the vent died. The eruption then returned to Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć, where flank vents on the west and southwest sides of the cone constructed a new lava shield. Soon lava tubes were feeding lava from the vents to the ocean, with few surface flows in between. The flank vents have held center stage ever since, with the exception of a two-month pause in activity, early in 1997, which followed a brief fissure eruption in NńĀpau Crater, a short distance southwest of Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć.
On the evening of January 29, 1997, a series of earthquakes struck Kńęlauea's east rift zone. Deep within the rift zone, magma was escaping from the conduit leading to the Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć vent, cutting off the supply to the ongoing eruption. The lava pond at Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć drained, and residents 10 miles (16 km) away heard a low, rumbling roar as the crater floor dropped 500 feet (150 m) and the west wall of the Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć cone collapsed. A few hours later, as magma found a new path to the surface, the ground cracked in nearby NńĀpau Crater, and lava fountains lit up the night sky. However, activity in this area was short-lived, and the center of activity soon shifted back to Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć.

As of January 2007, 3.1 cubic km of lava had covered 117 km2 (45 sq mi) and added 201 hectares (500 acres) to Kńęlauea's southern shore. The new shoreline was 15.6 km (9.7 mi) long. The lava flows have destroyed 189 structures and covered 14 km (8.7 mi) of highway with as much as 35 m (115 ft) of lava.
In 2007, after a cluster of earthquakes, activity in Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć subsided and the crater floor collapsed, with no incandescence visible in the crater after the end of August. Lava began emerging from a series of cracks in the northeast rift zone and spread slowly east and south as a perched flow, with slow advances of  Ľa ĽńĀ. The flow spread mostly over flows of 1983‚Äď1986, with minor incursions into adjoining forests.

In late July 2008, additional flows extended from the eastern vents of Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć and in October multiple new fissures opened along the length of the tube expanding into Royal Gardens Subdivision and covered a large area of the coastal flats in November 2008.
On March 5, 2011, the floor of the Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć crater deflated, then collapsed. Two hours later, a new eruption occurred in Kńęlauea's middle east zone, between Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć and Napau Crater. Lava fountains were reported to be 65 feet (20 m) high.
On March 26, 2011, lava began to refill the crater's floor, being visible in USGS HVO webcam. The USGS stated that the accumulation of lava had put the crater floor about 39 m (128 ft) below the eastern crater rim, as of June 1.
On September 21, 2011, lava in the west lava lake in Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć Crater fed a series of lava flows that traveled down the west flank of Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć during September 20‚Äď21. At about 0225 UTC on September 21, activity in the crater and overflows to the west suddenly decreased, as lava broke through the upper east flank of Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć, bypassing the crater. The new fissure fed a channelized  Ľa ĽńĀ lava flow that advanced rapidly downslope 2.5 km (1.6 mi) southeast. A second flow to the west of the first began the next day. In addition, a small pad of lava actively refilled the bottom of the drained east lava lake and small flows were barely active at the west edge of Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć Crater. The channelized  Ľa ĽńĀ lava flow reached 3.7 km (2.3 mi) long on 23 September and then stalled within the Kahauale Ľa Natural Area Reserve. Most of the active lava spread south and west of Pu Ľu Halulu (1.3 km or 0.8 mi northeast of Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć) during 23‚Äď27 September. Minor lava activity resumed within Pu Ľu  ĽO Ľo Crater with short lava flows issuing from the base of the east wall on 25 September and from the west wall base during 25‚Äď26 September. The crater floor of Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć slowly subsided. Lava activity resumed within the east lake on 26 September. The floor of the crater continued to subside during 26‚Äď27 September, opening up cracks in the north crater floor.
The 2011 activity ultimately destroyed all remaining homes in the Royal Gardens Subdivision. Jack Thompson evacuated his home March 2, 2012. His evacuation and return to the property was documented by Leigh Hilbert.

2014‚Äď2016: Lava flow threatens PńĀhoa
On June 27, 2014, new vents opened on the northeast flank of the Pu Ľu  ĽŇĆ ĽŇć cone that fed a narrow lava flow to the east-northeast. On August 18, the flow entered a ground crack, traveled underground for several days, then resurfaced to form a small lava pond. The sequence was repeated twice more over the following days with lava entering other cracks and reappearing farther downslope. In this way, the flow had advanced approximately 13.2 km (8.2 mi) from the vent, or to within 1.3 km (0.81 mi) of the eastern boundary of the Wao Kele o Puna Forest Reserve, by the afternoon of September 3. Advancing to the northeast at intermittent rates, the flow had entered the village of PńĀhoa and was within 25 meters (27 yards) of the waste recycling center on October 31.
In December 2014, a sustained lava breakout from this lava flow (informally named the "June 27 flow" by Hawaiian Volcano Observatory scientists) threatened to enter the town of PńĀhoa, and to cut Highway 130, the only route into and out of Lower Puna. As a result, work was begun to reopen Chain of Craters Road, initially as a one-lane gravelled surface, and to make Railroad Avenue and Government Beach Road usable as emergency routes. However, the flow stopped just short of entering PńĀhoa.
By March 2015, the June 27 flow retreated to within 6 kilometers of Pu‚Äėu ‚ÄėŇĆ‚ÄėŇć, greatly reducing the threat to PńĀhoa. Lava from the June 27 flow remained active in this area through the remainder of 2015.
On May 24, 2016, a new vent opened on the east flank of Pu‚Äėu ‚ÄėŇĆ‚ÄėŇć that cut the supply of lava to the June 27th flow, which became inactive by June 8, 2016. Lava from the new vent, informally named the "61g" flow by the Hawaiian Volcano Observatory, flowed south and began entering the ocean at Kamokuna on July 26, 2016. Lava from the 61g flow covered part of the regraded (but never reopened) new section of the Chain of Craters Road. The road is kept flat for use by authorities, but is no longer a public thoroughfare.

2018: Activity at Pu‚Äėu ‚ÄėŇĆ‚ÄėŇć stops; eruption shifts to the Lower East Rift Zone
On April 30, 2018, Pu‚Äėu ‚ÄėŇĆ‚ÄėŇć's crater floor collapsed as magma drained from the area beneath the cone. Following the collapse, seismicity and ground deformation increased downrift of Pu‚Äėu ‚ÄėŇĆ‚ÄėŇć. On May 3, fissures opened up at the Leilani Estates, marking the beginning of the 2018 lower Puna eruption. Through the rest of 2018, Pu‚Äėu ‚ÄėŇĆ‚ÄėŇć continued to experience intermittent collapses.
In January 2019, Hawaiian Volcano Observatory scientists stated that, considering historical data of prior rift zone eruptions and criteria used by the Global Volcanism Program to determine the end of an eruption, the Pu‚Äėu ‚ÄėŇĆ‚ÄėŇć eruption was most likely over.
